author: @Patriarchtech
text: Robert with your expertise in batteries and capacitors, I was wondering if it would make sense to talk a bit about load and torque. I watch tons of videos and every new attempt to present a generator always gets lost or "ground down" to use your terms, when it comes to handling a load. I think people are to lazy to understand load balancing and the need for shunts. A modern battery can handle all the torque in the world, parallel them with a capacitor bank and the sky is the limit. These hints you give in various projects very much goes in line with this. If you have constant generation albeit small then why the torque efficiency talk? Why do people insist on measuring the efficiency of a machine by its center axel while trying to stop it? The fastest moving physical objects man has created did the exact opposite (Voyager space probes). My point is ongoing generation is key and buying a 10kwh Tesla battery bank is the same as buying a bigger wallet really(and thinking it will refill itself)

author: @hissst69
text: Aw3some, So glad you made it a test tool :) I do one similar but its electrical not hand crank which just helps show everyone else efficiency numbers, for myself, hand crank is waaaay cooler :)

author: @wongowonga
text: Just to throw an idea in the mix. Not sure if its feasible but thought it worth a mention. Does one of the pullies that overlaps the aluminum disk spin in the opposite direction to the aluminum?  If so, it would be awesome if you could fit some slim line coils to the over lapping plastic pully you could generate twice, gaining extra benefit from the contrarotation? (if they would fit).  I can see a possible issue being the output connection from the extra coils getting tangled as the wheel spins. Although there maybe a way to resolve that issue that I haven't thought of. I'm fairly new to all this so please forgive me if I sound naïve. It would also be interesting if you could take this extra power (if it works) and somehow feed it back into the pulley system to assist with the crank winding. Making it super easy and light to turn? Anyway, I could be miles of the mark, just my ponderings.

replies.author: @wongowonga
replies.text: Upon thinking further, I realise that the extra power probably couldn't be fed back in because of the extra resistance created by the coils would effectively null it out. Could it still be usable power though? It does seem impractical but just trying to think outside of the box.

replies.author: @wongowonga
replies.text: O.K so upon even further thinking, the coils would unbalance the wheel (unless perfectly aligned). Probably not at all practical, maybe electrostatic gen using the principles I mentioned above? With your ink? probably little gain but some?

author: @jamest.5001
text: You got me wanting to collect about a dozen of these fan motor windings . and put them around a disc brake rotor with magnets on both sides, something like 1" cube neo. N52 Magnets, clearance the iron stators and rewind them with something like 18-20 gauge wire, for higher current, and more turns for voltage, build a axial flux wind turbine, soon as it gets past the cogging. It should make a fair amount of power, I'd hope for about 60v and about half the current capability in 20 mph einds. Arrange for 3 phase, it could be interesting,  I'd like to see how coreless coils compaired!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: I like the testing coils and magnetic field idea, that mate is very handy. But having said that, it would be hard to test it with a hand crank, I would put a variable speed motor where the crank is so that I get the same speed with each test. This way you could swap the magnets and coils around with the exact same speed each test to find out which coil and magnet will work the best with each other.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's a nice idea mate - you know me I go for simple but you are 100% right I think

replies.author: @jasonwitt8619
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering LOL, I don't know about that Rob, I think you sir might be anything but simple. I don't know too many people that would attempt half the stuff you do, but taking apart the exercise bike is nowhere near simple and then turning it into a generator \ magnet tester, you are on a whole other level than simple, LOL

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Thank you once again Rob. I have a couple of those fans and I always suspected that the coil would put out more volts than smaller coils.
I see these coils and the amount of turns in the coil and immediately start thinking about the turns in a transformer because this always depends on wire diameter and the number of turns (or ratio).

replies.author: @McRootbeer
replies.text: I read a paper on flash graphene.  It sounds like packing carbon materials (like coffee grounds) between 2 electrodes in a test tube, and hitting it with 90v for 1-3 sec produced high quality graphene.  I've been hoping Rob would make a video on it.  It sounds way too easy.

replies.author: @NoName1000No

author: @Scott_C
text: Great video as always Robert! With your previous work on generating fields with spinning objects and this one, combined with your love of steampunk, it got me thinking. Using a Crookes Radiometer, that black and white spinning scientific demonstration of thermal propulsion, Could you take those moving fins, magnetize or using your conductive paint make it generate electricity. Almost like a directly driven solar generator.
